mission.title: Install and Configure Network Service Mesh on Kubernetes
mission.description: Network Service Mesh provides a way to manage and connect network services in a Kubernetes environment. Installing it allows you to leverage its capabilities for service connectivity and management.

steps.title: Add the Network Service Mesh Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Helm repository for Network Service Mesh to your Helm client.
```bash
helm repo add networkservicemesh https://networkservicemesh.github.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Network Service Mesh
steps.description:
Install the Network Service Mesh using Helm, specifying the desired namespace.
```bash
helm install nsm networkservicemesh/nsm --namespace nsm-system --create-namespace --version v1.16.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the pods are running correctly in the nsm-system namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n nsm-system
```

steps.title: Post-install configuration
steps.description:
Configure resource limits for the Network Service Mesh components.
```bash
kubectl set resources deployment nsm --namespace nsm-system --limits=cpu=500m,memory=512Mi --requests=cpu=250m,memory=256Mi
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Network Service Mesh release from your cluster.
```bash
helm uninstall nsm --namespace nsm-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions that were created during the installation.
```bash
kubectl delete crd networks.networkservicemesh.io
kubectl delete crd networkservices.networkservicemesh.io
kubectl delete crd endpoints.networkservicemesh.io
```

uninstall.title: Remove the namespace
uninstall.description:
Delete the namespace used for Network Service Mesh.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace nsm-system
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to back up your current configuration and resources.
```bash
kubectl get all -n nsm-system -o yaml > nsm-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Ensure you have the latest version of the Helm charts.
```bash
helm repo update
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Network Service Mesh
upgrade.description:
Perform the upgrade to the latest version of Network Service Mesh.
```bash
helm upgrade nsm networkservicemesh/nsm --namespace nsm-system --version v1.16.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the pods are running correctly after the upgrade.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n nsm-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in Pending state
troubleshooting.description:
This may occur due to insufficient resources in your cluster. Check the events for the pods.
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n nsm-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Network connectivity issues
troubleshooting.description:
If services cannot communicate, verify that the Network Service Mesh components are running and check their logs.
```bash
kubectl logs <nsm-pod-name> -n nsm-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter an error stating that the Helm release is not found, ensure that you are using the correct namespace and release name.
```bash
helm list --namespace nsm-system
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Network Service Mesh will have all pods running in the nsm-system namespace, and you will be able to manage network services effectively. You should also see no errors in the logs of the Network Service Mesh components.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add networkservicemesh https://networkservicemesh.github.io/charts
helm repo update
helm install nsm networkservicemesh/nsm --namespace nsm-system --create-namespace --version v1.16.0
kubectl set resources deployment nsm --namespace nsm-system --limits=cpu=500m,memory=512Mi --requests=cpu=250m,memory=256Mi

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
